topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on my phone, a stark white intrusion in my otherwise muted afternoon. It was a package, addressed to the old apartment number, 4B. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I watched the box sit on my worn welcome mat, the plain brown cardboard seeming to mock my current, slightly cluttered existence. Inside, I could almost picture what it contained—perhaps new furniture, or maybe just a fresh start for someone who hadn't bothered to clean up after themselves. I picked it up, the paper rough beneath my fingertips, and felt a sharp, bitter taste rise in my throat. I carried it upstairs, the weight of it pressing down on my shoulders as I navigated the familiar hallway. Every glance toward the closed door of 4B felt like a painful reminder of a life I wasn't part of.

[story 2]

When the courier dropped the parcel off, the sound of the heavy door closing echoed in the quiet apartment. I stood by the door, my hands instinctively gripping the frame until my knuckles turned white. The contents—a box labeled with elegant, unfamiliar script—felt like an accusation. I paced the small living room, my steps quick and jerky, my gaze constantly flicking toward the entryway. I traced the outline of the box with my eyes, imagining the person who had received it, someone whose life seemed infinitely richer, more organized. A low, frustrated sigh escaped my lips, a sound heavy with unspoken comparison. I couldn't bring myself to open it immediately; the anticipation of what lay inside was almost unbearable, a tangible measure of everything I felt was missing.

[story 3]

The delivery was an unwelcome punctuation mark in my day. A package meant for the previous occupant of this unit. I stared at the label, the handwriting suggesting a life lived with more intention, more possessions. A strange heat began to prickle on my neck. I walked to the kitchen, running a hand over the cool surface of the counter, trying to steady my breathing. My jaw tightened. It wasn't just the contents of the box that bothered me; it was the implication—that someone else had successfully curated a space, a narrative, that I hadn't managed to build. I retreated to my room, the package resting on my desk like a stone. I picked up a pen, but the ink seemed to swim before my eyes. All I could think was that they had the key to a better chapter, and I was just standing outside the door.
